answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any difficulty breathing or chest pain associated with your back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a7: I tried tylenol and it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q8: Do you have any history of kidney problems, recent urinary symptoms, or abdominal issues?
answers.intake_q9: Any history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q10: Can you describe the quality of your back pain and if anything makes it better or worse?
answers.intake_a10: dull ache and it's better with excellent posture and worse if I'm leaning on the area sitting

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull aching pain localized to the left side of the lower rib cage. She denies any recent trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, chest pain, or respiratory symptoms. There is no numbness, tingling, or weakness in the legs or feet. The pain worsens with leaning on the affected area while sitting and improves with maintaining excellent posture. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. There is no history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain likely related to postural strain or localized soft tissue irritation in the left lower rib cage area. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or myofascial pain 
2. Costochondritis or rib dysfunction 
3. Intercostal nerve irritation 
4. Early presentation of visceral pathology such as renal or splenic issues (less likely given absence of systemic symptoms)

P: 
- Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain; max 3 grams/day (adjust for liver disease).
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated 
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if history of renal disease, liver disease, or gastrointestinal bleeding; use lowest effective dose for shortest duration 
- Return precautions: Return if pain worsens, develops neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), fever, unexplained weight loss, or new chest pain or difficulty breathing 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flag symptoms; consider imaging or labs if symptoms persist or worsen

evidence.title: value is what Coveo indexes and uses as the title in Search Results.--> Page Not Found | ACOG
Ventricular Septal Defect - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf
evidence.snippet: Ventricular septal defects (VSDs) can be either congenital or acquired. The acquired form, referred to as ventricular septal rupture, occurs spontaneously and is associated with severe ischemic or inflammatory conditions. Congenital VSDs are the most common cardiac anomaly in children and are the second most common congenital abnormality in adults, following the bicuspid aortic valve.[1]
